sentences.text: Yes.
I'm a barber, and I worked in barber shop.
Oh, now, the year 1939.
We changed altogether.
What had happened all of sudden it was on a Friday, September 1st, 1939, where the army of Germany crossed the border of Poland, and they went into Poland.
And the first city they went in was the city I was in it, Czestochowa, because this is on the border of the German country.
Well, we-- that was in the morning we start running around to get-- to get, I think, and to run somewhere farther away from the German border.
But before we started anything, the German were already in the city.
We couldn't go nowhere.
And it's still on the afternoon of that day, we left this city of Czestochowa and we went outside about [INAUDIBLE], about 40 kilometers.
That means me, my mother, my little brother.
My older brother got married.
He was with wife.
He had a child.
His wife was also pregnant at that time, the nine-- in the nine month.
And they didn't go.
So we left.
We went to a place called Radomsko, which is not far away from Czestochowa.
We got over there, and they start bombing the place.
And before we looked around, the German were all over.
And we were far away from home.
So we decided to go back where we have a still a little apartment and our belonging and to stay home, because it's no use to run away where they reaching us before even we got there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We went back home.
That was Friday.
And Saturday was a little bit quiet.
And Sunday after dark, they started coming more and more, not only from the German army, also from the party, like the black SS.
They wore black uniforms and red uniforms with the arm bands with the swastika.
Monday morning, they started to take out all the Jews from the part of the Jewish quarters, which wasn't exactly the Jewish quarter, because over there, also, non-Jews lived over there too.
But they took all of them out.
They took them to various places, like a place where they had the Polish army left over there.
Myself and thousands of other people, they took into a church, a very big church.
No.
Where they where, my brother was a different place.
My mother was a different place.
I was there myself.
And over there, they started going into the church, putting the ammunition, killing people.
Huge number of people are killed going in to the church, in the church.
Shooting.
And over the-- over the-- a couple of days.
And all of a sudden, out-- they let us out from the church.
And we went back home.
Hm?
Men.
Just men, yes.
They-- they didn't have to lock us inside because at the front of the door, there was SS.
So there was no use to-- to lock us.
Nobody-- nobody anyway could escape.
No.
They didn't give us any food.
But we had over there priest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he did for us a lot, a lot.
He said, don't be ashamed.
Use any place for a toilet.
You get water, whatever we can do, we will help you.
His name was Frubleski.
Yeah.
He was very, very, very fine man from the priest.
In Catholics, he was one of the finest which I know of.
He was inside with us, yes.
I don't know.
No I wouldn't know.
I don't know.
Then we went home.
And we started to build up a little bit of our-- our life.
I went back to work.
And everything was like little bit normal.
Yes.
My mother was back home, and my brother was back home.
My little brother was also back.
And we started to live by day by day.
And the life was very, very hard because money we didn't have.
And the prices started to rise, like a bread.
We paid for a bread $0.50 or $1 for 2 pound.
It grew to about $3, $4.
It was very hard, that time.
Oh, later on, yes.
Later on, should I say, you know, few weeks later was an order.
Jewish people can go out up till-- excuse me-- up to 7 o'clock.
You got to be inside between 7:00 and 7:00 in the morning.
They couldn't go out.
Any-- anyway, any place, they couldn't leave the houses, not even emergency.
All that was, oh, little by little was going on like that.
And then come deference orders.

Deference orders means like taking out Jewish people from better apartments and giving away to Volksdeutsche, which Volksdeutsche means people who lived in Poland, and their great-great-grandparents used to be Germans.
They called them Volksdeutsche.
All they took the apartment for themself.
Or they give those good apartments for Polish people, which they work together with the Nazis.
And it was like every day was worst and worst because they cordoned off the Jewish people.
It wasn't a ghetto yet.
[INAUDIBLE] so a high number of Jewish people, which they came in the Germans.
You got to leave, take in five minutes whatever you can, and out.
So they have to leave everything and just to go.
Where could they go?
Some of them, they had some relatives in the poor quarters.
And some, they went into just to public places, like [GERMAN], which supported the poor people.
And that-- that was very, very hard.
Then they started taking away clothes, taking away fur, especially.
Fur is not allowed ever at all for Jewish people.
Wherever they caught somebody wearing a fur coat or even small things, they killed them, because was an order to give that up.
They needed that for the army.
That-- that started in the end of 1939.
I think around December was an order to wear blue and white arm bands with a Star of David.
In the front.
No, no.
Excuse me.
On the arm, on the left arm.
No, you could remove it, because sewing on was too much, because people wore different clothes.
So we just took off the armband and put on-- make those with a little [INAUDIBLE].
And it was also an order whoever will not wear that thing and will go out will be shot.
And they did it.
So that was the order at that time.
Until [INAUDIBLE] they start Jewish press, there was no paper.
To know anything was not allowed.
To gather this-- to gather, let's say, more than four or five people at one time, it was not allowed, because they are called a meeting.
So was not allowed to even to talk to each other about what had happened.
But that was going on until about '41.
And '41, they created the big ghetto in Czestochowa.
The ghetto was in the poor place, in a poor neighborhood.
They opened up, let's say, about six, seven streets, which was for the Jewish people.
And in so many days, two or three days, they have to move out from that apartment and to move into the Jewish quarters.
No, I lived in that place.
I lived in that poor neighborhood, so we stayed in.
But it happened also that the people that came from the better neighborhood, which they have to leave their places, we had to take in.
Somebody had a bigger apartment, they were assigned two, three families got to take in.
So naturally, it was very crowded.
Well, with us was about seven people in one room.
Oh, we slept.
We slept on the floor, bed, but everybody slept.
We slept.
And it was-- it was a very hard time at time.
Some of them, you know, they brought in with them some money.
Some of them, they did not.
And we had to share whatever we could.
And I would say at the time of sorrow, like that, what we had, the people were very generous helping each other.
There were not mean, like it happened sometime.
So did the best to-- to survive.
It was--
It was-- yes.
To be exactly, I think, if I'm not mistaken, it was in May, 1941.
No, no.
Guarded-- guarded by the Jewish police, Polish police, Ukrainian, and Germans.
The beginning was mainly from Czestochowa.
But after that, people came from smaller places around Czestochowa.
And we had to take them in.
It growed-- it grew the population from about 35 when the war started until over 50,000 when the ghetto was established.
When it started.
And when the ghetto was populated, the people around in the smaller places, we had over 50,000 Jewish people.
Yes.
Yeah, there was a Judenrat.
Of course there was a Judenrat.
Chosen by the Gestapo.
It happened always that he was people very well known.
Yes, I know-- I know, I would say, often.
President, who was by the name of [?
Kopinsky, ?] which were a very rich man, and a few more of them, a few doctors, a few more of them.
And I would say they're very, very nice people.
They could what they-- they did what they could.
But they couldn't do much.
No, the actually job was support the Germans, whatever the need.
They ordered them, let's say, contribution $100,000 so that they have to guard that.
They order them they need, let's say, a couple of thousand people to work.
They did Judenrat had to get those people.
Oh, the food situation was very bad.
We were starving almost, in the ghetto.
It was a rationing, from-- from the also from Judenrat.
We got little coupons.
But we couldn't do much with it.
Whatever we could.
Some bread, some potatoes.
Meat, we didn't see.
Yes.
Was a barber.
Jewish, and people, also.
Oh, I had a sign, let's say, from the Judenrat, a couple hundred people.
They were coming in with their coupons.
I had to cut their hair, free for nothing.
That was-- I would think that we were not taking out from the city and sent some places to get killed.
There was a-- there was a good job for us, because we were on the place.
No, no.
Yes.
Yes.
Some of them, they escaped.
And some of them, it happened they killed them.
Many times, by the Germans, many times.
And by the Ukraines, also.
Beating, cutting of the beard, cutting off the [INAUDIBLE], taking off the clothes.
Many, many of it.
No, because they're Jews.
Yes.
His name was [?
Dagenhardt. ?]
Dagenhardt, ?] he was the head man from the Gestapo.
And he was like the king of the ghetto.
No.
No.
At that time, no.
At that time, no.
We had later on.
No.
Very little.
Very little, beside-- beside reading.
And also, it was-- it was not allowed to have books, which they had some kind of political underground, like from socialist or Zionist, or other thing.
So the first thing was we got rid of the books.
I, myself, I must have, I would say, at least a couple thousand of books.
You had to rid.
A girlfriend?
Yes, I had.
Yes.
Yes.
Were you married?
I married that girlfriend.
In the ghetto, when the ghetto started.
Her name was Reizl.
Prepalitski. ?]
I married her inside the ghetto.
A rabbi.
Yes, we had a ceremony.
We had a small wedding, just the family.
No, we had no celebration.
Where-- where could we celebrate?
We couldn't celebrate.
No, I took-- I took a room. for a lady that she had an empty place for her children [INAUDIBLE].
And I moved in.
But we stayed over there not too long.
I would say about five, six months.
And then it started.
They-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they take out the Jewish people and to send them away.
We-- yes.
The first one started on September the 22nd, 1942.
Oh, what I remember of that, the first thing was that all of a sudden, all the starting from 1939 up to '42, there were no lights at night in the streets.
And all of a sudden, not just that night, just before September the 22nd, all the lights were on.
And police and Gestapo and all kind from them surrounded the ghetto.
And they called the lowest streets, one, two, out from the houses.
And I was between that too.
They took us out at that time.
Me, at that time was already my wife, and I had a small baby from four weeks.
My baby's name was after mine father, Will.
And they took out 6-- over 6000 Jewish people, man, woman, young, old.
And we went to the railroad station.
It happened that coming to the railroad station, all the train cars, they're full.
They couldn't push in anymore.
And they order us to go back to our home.
The 6,000 they left, you went home.
Yes.
There was a hospital.
No, I just mentioned before even in a-- in an emergency, nobody could go out from the house.
My wife, she was at that-- she was pregnant.
She-- she's supposed to give birth, but she couldn't go out to the hospital until 7 o'clock in the morning.
And she was suffering the whole night until after 7:00.
I was allowed to go out and to take her to the hospital.
And that--
The hospital was inside the ghetto.
Inside, yes.
It was a small-- it wasn't the big Jewish hospital what we had in-- in our-- in our town.
And over there, she had the baby.
She had a big boy, very big baby for that, you know?
For people they had nothing to eat, it was at that time big baby, about seven pound.
Hm, yes.
Yes, yes, were circumcision to the-- to the eight days, the way he was supposed to, yes.
We did it.
It was done, also, without the knowledge of the German authority.
Well, we were sent back from the railroad station.
People came over in [INAUDIBLE].
Oh, that what it is.
Now, you are released.
Now, you are supposed to be happy because they needed 6,000 people, and they got them.
And they would not take any more.
Yes?
Fine.
We gotten back to our little apartment, stayed there for couple of days.
But we thought some day, they-- it's the same thing, the Ukrainian and the Jewish police and the Polish and the Germans, they were still surrounded around.
And that happened three days later on a Friday, the first day of [?
Sohkot. ?]
Another.
At that time we were from the same street.
They took us out, and we were sent to the marketplace.
At the marketplace was a selection.
If you know what a selection is--
Now, selection is to select people to go to work or to go to debt.
And mostly, what they-- mostly what they did is they're separated.
The Germans.
For the Germans, which on top of them was this one [?
Dagenhardt. ?] I mentioned it before.
They separated people.
What that means is separate them.
Like they left a husband.
They took away the wife.
Left the wife, they took away the-- the husband.
Or they took away everybody.
In my thing, when I came to the place where this selection was held, they didn't select nobody.
Everybody to the railroad station.
So-- they needed more people.
So I went that time with my wife, my little brother.
My big brother with his wife and kid went already with the first aktion.
My mother went with me.
And my wife and my baby.
And also was--
My-- also was my wife's sister with her husband, also pregnant lady.
She was in nine month.
For eight years she couldn't have a baby.
They just at that time.
So went her the same.
And then I had some relatives also going with me the same one.
And we came into the railroad station.
They put them in the-- in the railroad train.
They locked us.
There was no train of people to ride, but they're just like for animals, I think.
Between-- in every car was between, I would say, 140 to 160 people.
No.
Hardly.
We just stayed, one-- one on top of the other.
And there was some job of it.
No, no food, no water.
